_meta.notes: Chapter 1 Lesson 6. Previously taught: kumusta, mabuti, ikaw, magandang, umaga, gabi, po, naman, sige, oo, hindi, opo.

cards.body: Gratitude is a big deal in Filipino culture. This is the word you'll need most.

example.note: Add po when thanking someone older or in authority.

cards.body: Walang anuman means 'you're welcome.' Literally: 'it's nothing.'

example.note: Like saying 'no problem' or 'don't mention it.'

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: Walang anuman literally translates to 'there is nothing' (walang = none, anuman = anything). Filipinos downplay their generosity.

cards.source_text: Someone holds the door for you.
cards.explanation: Salamat = thank you. Use it whenever someone helps you.

cards.body: Pasensya na means 'sorry' or 'excuse me.' It's asking for patience.

example.note: Works for bumping into someone, being late, or any small apology.

cards.source_text: You accidentally bump into someone at the store.
cards.explanation: Pasensya na po = sorry/excuse me (respectful). Perfect for small apologies.
Walang anuman = you're welcome. Literally 'it's nothing.'

cards.prompt: You're late to a meeting with your boss. Apologize respectfully.
cards.explanation: Pasensya na po = sorry (respectful). The go-to apology for authority figures.

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue. User must get ALL questions right to complete. Cannot finish lesson with outstanding wrong answers.
wrong_answer_tracking.wrong_count_usage: Feed to Gemini system prompt for AI Practice.

alam_mo_ba: Salamat may come from the Arabic 'salaam' (peace), brought by Muslim traders centuries before the Spanish arrived.
Pasensya comes from the Spanish 'paciencia' (patience). You're literally asking someone to be patient with you.
Many Filipinos also say 'thank you' in English even in full Tagalog conversations. Both are completely natural.
